figures.caption: Table 1: Task success rate (%) on dynamic scenes across constant-velocity and acceleration/deceleration regimes. Mean ± standard error over 5 seeds with 100 rollouts each.

Table 11: Task success rate (%) on the Plinko Drop stress test, parameterized by available time-to-exit window. This scenario explicitly violates the constant-acceleration motion assumption (Section 3) and is included to characterize graceful degradation. Mean ± standard error over 5 seeds with 100 rollouts each.

Figure 5: Complex dynamic simulation scenarios. The eight scenes test reactive contact, ballistic trajectories, multi-object selection, occlusion, mid-flight deflection, and language-conditioned target selection.

Figure 6: Stress-test simulation scenarios that exceed the low-order kinematic motion assumption from Section 3. Each ball undergoes multiple chaotic deflections; AHEAD degrades gracefully but does not match its in-distribution success rates.

Figure 7: Representative frames from the physical xArm 7 tasks. Left to right: conveyor pick-and-place variants, paddle-based projectile interception, projectile catching with a soft net mounted to the gripper.

Figure 3: AHEAD full architecture. The preprocessing stage computes optical flow between three consecutive frames using RAFT [28], pools to per-patch velocity, and finite-differences to recover per-patch acceleration. A frozen OpenVLA vision encoder produces N patch tokens, which are gated by language-guided cross-attention to select a task-relevant subset S of |S| tokens (typically 30 to 60 for N=196). A 4-layer transformer encoder compresses the selected motion-enriched tokens into a compact scene latent zt. The dynamic world model rolls forward via conditional flow matching with analytical kinematic conditioning Vk = V0 + A · k ·∆t, drawing S=5 samples per step. The rollout halts when inter-sample variance exceeds threshold τu, or at Kmax steps if the threshold is never crossed. The decoder reconstructs predicted patch tokens v̂(S) t+K , which are spliced into the full N -token grid (unselected positions pass through unchanged) and injected into the frozen OpenVLA backbone to produce the action at = π(v̂t+K , eℓ). Components trained from scratch (∼4.9M parameters total) are marked with a flame icon; components inherited frozen from OpenVLA are marked with a snowflake icon.

Table 7: Training data sources for world model pretraining and fine-tuning. The deployment setup uses Franka in simulation and a UFactory xArm 7 in the physical lab. Pretraining uses six public manipulation video sources spanning egocentric human activity and robot demonstrations from multiple embodiments.
Table 16: World model architecture ablation on constant-velocity scenes. Mean ± standard error over 5 seeds with 100 rollouts each.

Figure 2: AHEAD architecture. RAFT optical flow [28] provides per-patch velocity and acceleration. A Language-and-motion saliency mask selects a task-relevant subset S of N patch tokens, which are encoded, rolled forward by a conditional flow-matching world model with analytical kinematic conditioning Vk = V0 + A · k · ∆t, decoded, and spliced back into the full token grid for the frozen action decoder. Flame icons mark trained components (∼4.9M parameters total); snowflakes mark components frozen from OpenVLA. Figure 3 in Appendix A shows the full breakdown including autoregressive rollout, adaptive halting, and feature alignment.
Table 13: Velocity model ablation on acceleration/deceleration scenes. Mean ± standard error over 5 seeds with 100 rollouts each.
